old_title: Iran-US war live: US launches latest strikes after service member dies in explosion
new_title: Iran war live: Trump 'planning for wider war' with Tehran
old_text:
The U.S. military began conducting another wave of strikes against Iran on Sunday evening, U.S. Central Command has announced, marking the ninth consecutive night of attacks by American forces.
"The strikes will continue degrading Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial vessels and civilian mariners transiting the Strait of Hormuz," officials said Sunday.
Returning from the World Cup final, President Donald Trump said the attacks on Iran were in honor of "great patriots" in the America military that were killed in action in recent days.
"We hit them very hard again tonight," Trump told reporters.
The missions come a day after a US service member was killed during the detonation of unexploded Iranian ordnance in northern Iraq.
On Friday, two US service members were killed and another went missing during an Iranian attack in Jordan. Military personnel have recovered remains that Trump suspects are those of a third service member killed in the strike.
Meanwhile, Tehran claimed on Sunday that two US-backed tankers have been involved in an "accident" while attempting to traverse the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said two ships had ignored warnings and tried to navigate the Strait via an "unsafe route" while two other vessels abandoned that route, the semi-official Tasnim news agency reported.
UK defence secretary pays tribute to US service personnel killed in Jordan
UK defence secretary Dan Jarvis has commented on the deaths of two US service personnel killed in action in Jordan, saying: "My thoughts are with the families and friends of the US service personnel killed and injured in Jordan."
Four service members were evacuated to Jordanian hospitals for treatment and one service member is missing.
US 'planning for a wider war' but lacks munitions: report
Sunday's round of US strikes on Iran likely won't be the last, amid renewed hostilities and a string of American military deaths.
"The US is planning for a wider war," an unnamed US official familiar with the Trump administration's strategy told The Washington Post.
The official added that these plans come despite dwindling stores of air defense and long-range munitions, as well as degraded capacities to surge troops and aircraft.
"We do not have enough to safely sustain operations, and I don't think the White House is aware of that," the official said.
Trump defends delays in ending Iran conflict: 'This is a far bigger job'
President Donald Trump on Sunday fired back at a journalist who asked about his prior claim the Iran conflict would be finished in a matter of weeks.
"This is a far bigger job that we're doing," the Republican said from the tarmac as he returned from the World Cup final.
"It's really not the same thing," he added of the evolving mission in the Iran conflict, which began in February. "What we're doing now is we're ending any chance where they could have a nuclear missile."
The US previously claimed its June 2025 strikes on Iran "obliterated" the country's nuclear program.
On Sunday, the president painted a modestly different picture of Tehran's military capacities.
"They've lost everything, almost, military," he said. "They've got very little left. They've got some missiles. They've got some drones. They've got some manufacturing ability, not much. But we control the Strait. They don't control anything."
UAE strongly condemns the renewed Iran attacks
The United Arab Emirates issued a further statement on Sunday condemning the escalating strikes by Iran on Gulf states and Jordan.
In a post on X, the ministry of foreign affairs said that the "hostile attacks" on Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan "constitute a flagrant violation of the sovereignty of the brotherly nations and a threat to their security and stability".
Trump claims Iran strikes in honor of slain US service members
President Donald Trump provided a brief update on Sunday about the latest round of strikes against Iran as he headed back to the White House after attending the World Cup final.
"We hit them very hard again tonight, and we did that in honor of the probably three - it's probably three, as opposed to two - great patriots," Trump said from the tarmac.
U.S. Central Command has announced three service member deaths in recent days, including two military members dead after an Iranian attack on Friday in Jordan and a third fatality in Iraq as personnel sought to disable unexploded Iranian ordnance.
A fourth service member is missing as a result of the attack in Jordan.
"We feel very badly, but you know those great people, great patriots, were out there fighting [so] that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon," Trump said of the deaths.
The resulting retaliatory strikes have left Iran "very, very badly damaged," Trump added, though he often insists Iran has been all but decimated.
Explosions heard in five Iranian cities as US signals latest barrage: reports
Explosions were heard across at least five Iranian cities today, according to the semi-official Iranian news agency Tasnim, as the US announced another night of attacks.
Witnesses and local reporters told Tasnim of explosions in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini.
US Central Command said earlier Sunday that renewed strikes began around 7 p.m. Eastern time.
Rubio meets Lebanon's president to discuss Israel-Lebanon deal
Marco Rubio met with Lebanese president Aoun on Sunday to discuss the implementation of a US-brokered framework between Israel and Lebanon.
"The Secretary commended the courage of the Government of Lebanon, under President Aoun's leadership, for their determined effort to reclaim Lebanon's sovereignty, disarm (Hezbollah) and dismantle its terrorist infrastructure, and move towards peace," State Department spokesperson Tommy Pigott said in a statement.
US announces ninth consecutive round of daily strikes on Iran
American forces began striking targets in Iran on Sunday evening, the U.S. military announced, marking the ninth consecutive night of attacks.
"CENTCOM began conducting a new wave of strikes against Iran at 7 p.m. ET today for the ninth consecutive night," U.S. Central Command said in a statement on X. "The strikes will continue degrading Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial vessels and civilian mariners transiting the Strait of Hormuz."
Marco Rubio meets with Lebanese president in Washington
US secretary of state Marco Rubio met with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun in Washington, a state department official told AFP news agency.
President Aoun will reportedly meet Donald Trump on Tuesday and present him with a plan on how to disarm Hezbollah and secure Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon.
In a post on X, Lebanon's presidency said on Sunday: "President Joseph Aoun arrived at the building of the US department of state for a meeting with secretary of state Marco Rubio, thereby concluding his official engagements in Washington."
In depth: Nigerian children pay the price for the Iran war as malnutrition and poverty surge
Maryam Aminu was hardly surprised when the last of her six children was diagnosed with malnutrition in April for a second time. She was barely feeding the 18-month-old regularly, let alone with nutritious foods, writes Ope Adetayo.
Although the family in northwest Nigeria has struggled with economic hardship, their situation deteriorated after February, when her husband, Shehu Aminu, lost his job as a taxi driver due to a spike in the retail price of petrol caused by the war in Iran.
"When she was diagnosed the second time, even though I suspected it, I was sad and angry because I knew why," Aminu said in the living area of their unvarnished two-bedroom house in the quiet town of Kware, Sokoto, as ash from the coal stove billowed into the room. "Times are tough, and the food is not consistent."
Children relapsing into malnutrition has become an increasingly common occurrence in the state and across northern Nigeria, according to local health and aid workers, who cite knock-on effects from the Iran war.
Northern Nigeria, one of the world's poorest regions, is already under pressure from an insurgency crisis. Now, the conflict in the Middle East has worsened food security for millions of people living in poverty, especially children.
Read the full story here:
new_text:
The United States is said to be preparing for greater escalation with Iran following days of strikes as both sides wrestle for control of the Strait of Hormuz.
An American official familiar with internal discussions told the Washington Post that the US is still "planning for a wider war" by rushing military aircraft to the region as hopes of a return to diplomacy fade.
The official, speaking on condition of anonymity, warned that US operations would be limited by low stockpiles of air defences and long-range munitions, however.
The US will also struggle to send troops and aircraft to the region due to battle damage, the official said.
US forces hit Iran for a ninth consecutive day on Monday as Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said they had targeted American assets in Jordan, Kuwait and Syria.
Sirens also sounded in Bahrain on Monday morning, as the Kuwaiti army said it was intercepting hostile drones in an Iranian attack.
Iran still hearing from mediators: foreign ministry
Iran continues to receive messages from intermediaries despite the apparent halt to talks aimed at ending the war, the foreign ministry said today.
Spokesperson Esmail Baghaei rejected the dichotomy of war or diplomacy and said Iran would use both as a "tool" to pursue its national interests.
Iran to 'pursue diplomacy based on its national interests'
Tehran pursues war and diplomacy based on its national interests, Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson said on Monday, after being asked whether the door to talks with the U.S. was now closed.
"I reject the duality of either war or diplomacy. Diplomacy is a tool through which we pursue our national interests, just like war," Esmaeil Baghaei said.
Recap: Two US service members killed by Iranian attacks in Jordan, military says
At least two U.S. service members were killed in action while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks in Jordan, marking the first American deaths in the aftermath of a tenuous ceasefire agreement earlier this year, according to U.S. Central Command.
Another service member is missing after Friday's attacks, according to the Department of Defense.
Oil prices up 2 per cent as Iran and US inch closer to all out war
Oil prices jumped more than 2% on Monday, with Brent crude above $90 a barrel as the U.S. and Iran moved closer to resuming an all-out war.
Brent crude, the international standard, rose 2.6% to $90.40 per barrel and benchmark U.S. crude climbed 2.2% to $83.58 per barrel.
Early Monday, the U.S. announced more attacks for a ninth straight night. Iran has been responding to strikes by hitting U.S.-allies across the Middle East.
"The U.S. and Iran continue to exchange strikes, which are proving to be deadly for both sides," ING commodities strategists Warren Patterson and Ewa Manthey wrote in a commentary Monday.
"If this escalation goes unchecked, we could return to an environment of wide-scale attacks across the Persian Gulf."
Read the full story:
Latest: US and Iran trade strikes for a ninth day
Here's the latest on Monday morning:
- The United States said it had struck Iran for a ninth consecutive day.
- Explosions were heard in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini, according to Iran's Tasnim news agency.
- The IRGC said meanwhile that they had targeted US aircraft in Jordan, as well as US military assets and equipment in Kuwait and in Syria. Sirens also sounded in Bahrain.
- The UKMTO reported on an attack on a ship off Oman. It was not immediately reported what hit it, but the crew made it off safely.
- Oil rose by more than 2 per cent on Monday as both sides inched closer to all out war.
Trump 'planning return to wider war': US official
An American official familiar with internal talks tells the Washington Post that the US is preparing for a return to a "wider war" following days of escalatory strikes.
The official cautions that any operations will be limited by stockpile shortages of air defences and long-range munitions, however.
"We do not have enough to safely sustain operations, and I don't think the White House is aware of that," the official says.
New: Vessel struck with unknown projectile off Oman, says UKMTO
The UKMTO agency is reporting that a vessel has been struck off Oman's Kumzar.
They say it was hit by an as yet unknown projectile, and that the crew has safely abandoned the ship.
The vessel is currently adrift and the fire on board is yet to be extinguished, they say.
Gulf states intercept drones and missiles as attacks intensify
Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan reported intercepting Iranian drones and missiles as attacks across the region intensified.
Bahrain's ministry of interior activated warning sirens and urged residents to "remain calm and head to the nearest safe place", while the US embassy in Manama warned American citizens that Iran may be planning attacks on the capital and advised them to take precautionary measures.
Kuwait's military said it was responding to a series of drone attacks, while Jordan reported intercepting at least three missiles, with another landing in an open area.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed it had targeted a weapons depot and air defence radar system in Kuwait, though Kuwaiti authorities have not confirmed those claims and instead said they were dealing with the aftermath of an attack on a power and water desalination facility.
The strike on civilian infrastructure has drawn condemnation from countries across the region.
Oil prices climb above $90 a barrel as US-Iran tensions escalate
Oil prices climbed above $90 a barrel as escalating US-Iran tensions disrupted concerns over crude supplies through the Strait of Hormuz, a key global energy route.
Brent crude rose more than 2 per cent to $90.19 a barrel, reaching its highest level since 11 June, while US West Texas Intermediate (WTI) also gained to $84.20 a barrel.
The latest surge followed the US carrying out a ninth consecutive night of strikes on Iran, along with reports of fresh Iranian attacks on Gulf states.
Shipping data shows traffic through Strait of Hormuz remains limited
Shipping data showed that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remained limited over the weekend as the US and Iran intensified their attacks, according to Sky News.
According to LSEG data, only four vessels crossed the strategic waterway on Sunday, down from eight the previous day.
Since Friday, at least three oil tankers and one Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) have entered the strait to load oil.
The LSEG data tracks the movement of various types of vessels, including container ships, bulk carriers, vehicle carriers, and tankers transporting oil, gas and chemicals.
